Chunk # 0.
ORIGINAL PAPER
Simultaneous isolation of pure and intact chloroplasts
and mitochondria from moss as the basis for sub-cellularproteomics
Erika G E Lang •Stefanie J Mueller •Sebastian N W Hoernstein •
Joanna Porankiewicz-Asplund •Marco Vervliet-Scheebaum •Ralf Reski
Received: 16 July 2010 / Revised: 16 September 2010 / Accepted: 24 September 2010 / Published online: 20 October 2010
/C211The Author(s) 2010 This article is published with open access at Springerlink.com
Abstract The moss Physcomitrella patens is increasingly
being used as a model for plant systems biology studies While genomic and transcriptomic resources are in place,tools and experimental conditions for proteomic studies
need to be developed In the present study we describe a
rapid and efﬁcient protocol for the simultaneous isolationof chloroplasts and mitochondria from moss protonema Routinely, 60–100 lg mitochondrial and 3–5 mg chloro-
plast proteins, respectively, were obtained from 20 g freshweight of green moss tissue Using 14 plant compartment
marker antibodies derived from seed plant and algal protein
sequences, respectively, the evolutionary conservation ofthe compartment marker proteins in the moss wasdemonstrated and purity and intactness of the extracted
organelles conﬁrmed This isolation protocol and these
validated compartment markers may serve as basis for sub-cellular proteomics in P patens and other mosses Keywords Organelles /C1Compartment marker /C1
Chloroplast proteins /C1Mitochondrial proteins /C1
Physcomitrella /C1Bryophyte

DOI 10.1007/s00299-010-0935-4patens has been established as a plant system to investigate
the evolution of stress adaptation (Frank et al 2007 ;
Khandelwal et al 2010 ) and of signalling events (Heintz
et al 2004 ,2006 ) in early land plants Along with these
studies a wide range of high-throughput molecular biology
tools has been developed and implemented in recent years
(Richardt et al 2007 ,2010 ) paving the way for the use of
this model organism for systems biology studies (Decker
et al 2006 ) Focussing on plant organelles in a moss such as P patens
can be of special interest to obtain information on the
evolution of metabolic compartmentalisation (Kopriva et al.2007 ; Wiedemann et al 2010 ), biosynthetic pathways
(Stumpe et al 2006 ) and protein sorting mechanisms
(Kiessling et al 2004 , Mitschke et al 2009 , Richter et al 2002 ) Of special interest are chloroplasts and mitochondria
as they are semi-autonomous organelles of endosymbiotic
origin with own DNA that encodes only for a small subsetof proteins localised to these organelles Hence, most of the
proteins are nuclear-encoded and have to be imported into
chloroplasts and mitochondria, respectively (Gray et al.1999 ; Reski 2009 ; Strittmatter et al 2010 ) The prediction
of sub-cellular protein localisation, however, is error prone
because the transit peptides are not well conserved (Bruce2001 ) and prediction algorithms are usually trained on the
basis of proteins from seed plants Experimental data sets
have shown that the tools currently available for the pre-diction of sub-cellular localisation can only identify about
50% of the proteins targeted to organelles (Heazlewood
et al 2004 ; Kleffmann et al 2004 ) These limitations can
only be overcome by the generation of species-speciﬁc
training data sets for the respective organelles, the data sets
being very much dependent on the speciﬁcity, i.e correctprediction of the protein localisation (Baginski and Gruissem
2004 ; Salvi et al 2008b ) The generation of reliable data sets
is, however, difﬁcult as contaminations with proteins fromother organelles and from the cytosol can never be ruled out
during the isolation of single organelles

summary:
Summary: Iteration 6
Simultaneous isolation of pure and intact chloroplasts and mitochondria from moss for sub-cellular proteomics.
- Moss Physcomitrella patens, a non-seed plant with a completely sequenced genome, serves as a model organism for plant systems biology studies.
- A rapid and efficient protocol for isolating chloroplasts and mitochondria from moss has been developed.
- Marker antibodies derived from seed plant and algal protein sequences validate the purity and intactness of the isolated organelles.
- The presence of conserved marker proteins across different plant species demonstrates the evolutionary conservation of sub-cellular compartments.
- Prediction algorithms trained on proteins from seed plants are used to determine the sub-cellular localization of nuclear-encoded proteins in chloroplasts and mitochondria.
- The isolation protocol and validated compartment markers provide a basis for sub-cellular proteomics in Physcomitrella patens and other mosses.
- Moss Physcomitrella patens is used as a model system to study the evolution of stress adaptation and signaling events in early land plants.
- High-throughput molecular biology tools have been developed for comprehensive analysis of molecular processes in moss.
- Genomic and transcriptomic resources are available for in-depth investigations into the biological processes of moss.
- Chloroplasts and mitochondria are semi-autonomous organelles with their own DNA, encoding only a small subset of proteins.
- Proteomic studies focus on sub-cellular proteomics, using compartment marker proteins to isolate and analyze chloroplasts and mitochondria.
- The isolation protocol and validated compartment markers ensure the purity and intactness of the extracted organelles.
- Evolutionary conservation of compartment marker proteins provides insights into the evolution of metabolic compartmentalization.
- Predicting sub-cellular protein localization relies on transit peptides, although they are not well conserved.
- Tools for predicting sub-cellular localization require the generation of species-specific training data sets.
- Contaminations with proteins from other organelles and cytosol during isolation can be minimized using plant compartment marker antibodies.
- Mosses serve as a non-seed plant model for studying plant systems biology, providing insights into stress adaptation and signaling events in early land plants.
- Chloroplasts and mitochondria are semi-autonomous organelles with their own DNA, encoding a small subset of proteins.
- Predicting sub-cellular protein localization is challenging due to the lack of well-conserved transit peptides.
- Marker antibodies derived from seed plant and algal protein sequences validate the purity and intactness of isolated organelles.
- Moss Physcomitrella patens is used as a model system to study the evolution of metabolic compartmentalization.

article:
Chunk # 1.
Many protocols for the isolation of plant organelles in
seed plants are established and have been used for sub-
sequent high-throughput shotgun proteomic studies of
Arabidopsis chloroplasts (Kleffmann et al 2004 ; Baginski
et al 2005 ) and mitochondria (Heazlewood et al 2004 ;
Millar et al 2001a ,b; Sweetlove et al 2007 ) or for
example, the analysis of mitochondria in rice (Heazlewoodet al 2003 ; Huang et al 2009 ) All these studies employ
density gradients for the puriﬁcation of organelles, some-
times combining it with free ﬂow electrophoresis (FFE) toseparate chloroplasts from mitochondria (Eubel et al 2007 ;
Huang et al 2009 ; Lee et al 2008 ) However, losses of
about 50% of the organelle material can occur (Eubel et al.2007 ), creating a need for the adaptation of existing pro-
tocols for each model species (Sweetlove et al 2007 ).For the moss P patens protocols for the isolation of
organelles via density gradients have been reported
(Kabeya and Sato 2005 ; Kasten et al 1997 ; Marienfeld
et al 1989 ) However, the moss material used in these
experiments was always subjected to protoplastation,
which besides from being a laborious and costly pre-
treatment of the material might also have an effect on thephysiological status of the cell and, hence, its proteome The aim of this study was to set up a protocol for the
simultaneous isolation of highly enriched fractions of pure
and intact chloroplasts and mitochondria from protonema
tissue of P patens  Integrity and purity of these fractions as
well as potential contaminations were assessed using a set
of plant compartment marker antibodies The protocol
presented here enables for the isolation of intact chloro-plasts and mitochondria and delivers protein yields that are
sufﬁcient for sub-cellular proteomic studies in Physcomit-
rella  Such studies can provide the basis for a large-scale
analysis of protein sorting mechanisms in moss and, in
addition, unravel the evolution of metabolic and biosyn-
thetic processes occurring in plant organelles Materials and methods
Plant material and growth conditions
Protonema of Physcomitrella patens (Hedw.) Bruch &
Schimp was cultured in modiﬁed liquid Knop medium
according to Reski and Abel ( 1985 ) containing 250 mg/l
KH
2PO4, 250 mg/l KCl, 250 mg/l MgSO 497H 2O,
1,000 mg/l Ca(NO 3)294H2O and 12.5 mg/l FeSO 49
7H2O (pH 5.8) Protonema ﬁlaments were disrupted with an Ultra-
Turrax (IKA, Staufen, Germany) at 18,000 rpm for 90 s
before inoculation Round-bottom ﬂasks containing 5 l ofmedium were inoculated with 0.3 g dry weight and aerated
with 0.3 vvm at 25 /C176C under long day conditions [16 h
light, 8 h dark, Osram TLD 36 W/25, 70 lmol/(m
2s)] After 7 days, moss was harvested and ﬁlter-dried using a
vacuum pump and a Bu ¨chner funnel Organelle isolation
All following steps were performed at 4 /C176C and, where
applicable, on wet ice Typically, two 5 l ﬂasks were used
per experiment, which corresponds to 20 g fresh weight of
moss The vacuum ﬁltrated protonema was chopped inorganelle isolation buffer [1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrroli-
done (PVPP), 300 mM
D-sorbitol, 50 mM HEPES, 2 mM
Na-EDTA, 1 mM MgCl 2and 0.1% BSA] in the presence
of a protease inhibitor [0.1% (v/v) Sigma Plant Protease
Inhibitor Cocktail P 9599] using a household vegetable206 Plant Cell Rep (2011) 30:205–215
123chopping device After 100–150 strokes with this device
the chopped moss was ﬁltered through 3 layers of Mira-
cloth (Calbiochem) The ﬁltrate was transferred to a 50 ml
centrifuge tube (Oak Ridge, Nalgene) and the chloroplastswere pelleted at 1,500 9gfor 10 min (Beckman Coulter
Avanti Centrifuge J-25, ﬁxed angle rotor Ja 25.50) The
supernatant was decanted into new centrifuge tubes andused for the isolation of mitochondria (see below) A workﬂow diagram outlining the isolation protocol is
given in Fig 1 Chloroplast isolationThe crude chloroplast pellet was resuspended in 3 ml
resuspension buffer (300 mM
D-sorbitol, 50 mM HEPES,
2 mM Na-EDTA, 1 mM MgCl 2and 0.1% BSA) using a ﬁne
paint brush The resuspended sample was carefully loaded
onto several Percoll density gradients (top to bottom: 5 ml10% Percoll, 5 ml 40% Percoll, 5 ml 80% Percoll) prepared
in resuspension buffer The Percoll gradients were centri-
fuged for 20 min at 16,000 9g Broken chloroplasts accu-
mulated at the 10–40% interface, while intact chloroplasts
formed a band at the 40–80% interface The fractions
containing intact chloroplast were collected and combinedusing a thin glass pipette The chloroplasts were washed
twice with three volumes of washing buffer (300 mM
D-sorbitol, 50 mM HEPES, 2 mM Na-EDTA, 1 mM
MgCl 2) and centrifuged for 10 min at 1,500 9g Organelle
purity was monitored via ﬂuorescence microscopy Pellets
were frozen at -80/C176C

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps for Isolation of Chloroplasts and Mitochondria from Protonema Tissue of P. patens:**

1. Protonema of Physcomitrella patens was cultured in modified liquid Knop medium.
2. Protonema filaments were disrupted using an Ultra-Turrax.
3. Round-bottom flasks containing medium were inoculated with the disrupted protonema.
4. After 7 days, the moss was harvested and filter-dried.
5. The moss was chopped in an organelle isolation buffer containing polyvinylpolypyrrolidone (PVPP), D-sorbitol, HEPES, Na-EDTA, MgCl2, and BSA.
6. A protease inhibitor cocktail was added to the buffer.
7. The chopped moss was filtered through Mira-cloth.
8. The filtrate was transferred to a centrifuge tube, and chloroplasts were pelleted by centrifugation.
9. The supernatant was decanted for the isolation of mitochondria.
10. The crude chloroplast pellet was resuspended in a resuspension buffer containing D-sorbitol, HEPES, Na-EDTA, MgCl2, and BSA.
11. The resuspended sample was loaded onto Percoll density gradients.
12. The Percoll gradients were centrifuged to separate broken chloroplasts and intact chloroplasts.
13. Intact chloroplasts formed a band at the 40-80% interface.
14. Fractions containing intact chloroplasts were collected and combined.
15. The chloroplasts were washed with a washing buffer and centrifuged.
16. Organelle purity was monitored via fluorescence microscopy.
17. Pellets were frozen at -80°C.

**Incomplete Protocol Parts or Unclear Steps:**
- The specific composition of the modified liquid Knop medium is not mentioned.
- The details of the disruption method using Ultra-Turrax are not provided.
- The specific details of the organelle isolation buffer, such as the concentration of each component, are not mentioned.
- The number of Percoll density gradients used is not specified.
- The specific centrifugation conditions for the Percoll gradients are not provided.
- The specific details of the washing buffer, such as the concentration of each component, are not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of chloroplasts and mitochondria from protonema tissue of P. patens. However, there are some missing protocol parts and unclear steps that need to be addressed for a complete understanding of the procedure.

summary: Summary (Version 4): A protocol was developed to isolate highly enriched fractions of pure and intact chloroplasts and mitochondria from Physcomitrella patens, a type of moss. The moss was subjected to organelle isolation using Percoll density gradients and specific buffers. The purity of the isolated organelles, including chloroplasts and mitochondria, was monitored using fluorescence microscopy. The protocol, adapted from existing methods, yielded high protein yields for large-scale analysis. These proteomic studies aimed to analyze protein sorting mechanisms and the evolution of metabolic and biosynthetic processes in plant organelles, providing valuable insights into their functions.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from the article:

1. Centrifuge the 1,500 9g supernatant at 3,000 9g for 5 min, followed by 6,000 9g for 5 min to pellet nuclei and cellular debris.
2. Decant the resulting supernatant to a new tube and centrifuge at 18,000 9g for 20 min to yield a greenish pellet enriched in mitochondria.
3. Carefully resuspend the pellet in 1 ml of washing buffer using a fine paint brush.
4. Combine several pellets in a potter to a final volume of 4.8 ml and homogenize with 10 strokes.
5. Add 100% Percoll to the sample to a final concentration of 20%.
6. Assemble a stepwise Percoll density gradient by overlaying 5 ml of 80% Percoll in washing buffer with 5 ml of 33% Percoll in washing buffer.
7. Transfer the homogenized sample in 20% Percoll on top of the gradient and centrifuge at 18,000 9g for 1 h.
8. Extract two pale whitish mitochondrial bands at the 20–33% interface and the 33–80% interface, referred to as bands M1 and M2, respectively.
9. Collect the mitochondria carefully using thin glass pipettes and remove Percoll by adding 15 ml of washing buffer, gently mixing, and centrifuging at 18,000 9g for 20 min, repeated twice.
10. The upper band yields a greenish, loose mitochondrial pellet, while the lower band forms a stable whitish pellet containing residual PVPP.
11. Freeze the pellets at -80°C.
12. Perform protein extraction from the frozen organelle pellets and protonema using lysis buffer and subsequently precipitate with methanol/chloroform.
13. Resuspend the protein pellet in urea buffer and measure the protein concentration.
14. Mix the protein with SDS sample buffer for SDS-PAGE.
15. Perform sequential chloroplast protein extraction to obtain a soluble fraction (Cs) and a chloroplast membrane fraction (Cm) using chloroplast protein extraction buffers I and II.
16. Prepare the soluble total protein fraction (Ts) using Tris-buffer and homogenize the frozen protonema with mortar and pestle.
17. Centrifuge the homogenate and precipitate the proteins with acetone.
18. Resuspend the protein pellet in lysis buffer.

Incompleteness:
- The article does not provide specific details for steps 12-18, such as the volumes of lysis buffer, methanol/chloroform, or acetone used.

Conclusion:
The protocol for mitochondrial isolation from the article provides a detailed step-by-step procedure. However, there are gaps in the protocol, particularly in the protein extraction steps, where specific volumes and details are not provided.

summary: Mitochondria were isolated using a protocol of Kabeya and Sato (2005) involving differential centrifugation and a Percoll density gradient. The supernatant was centrifuged to pellet nuclei and cellular debris, resulting in a greenish pellet enriched in mitochondria. The pellet was resuspended in washing buffer and combined with other pellets before homogenization. Percoll was added, and a stepwise density gradient was assembled. Two mitochondrial bands were extracted at the interfaces. The mitochondria were collected, freed of Percoll, and frozen. Protein extraction was performed using lysis buffer and precipitated with methanol/chloroform. Chloroplast protein extraction was also performed using a modified protocol from Molloy et al. (1998) to obtain soluble and membrane fractions.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Determine protein concentration of the fractions using the Bradford assay.
2. Mix the fractions with SDS sample buffer for SDS-PAGE Western blotting.
3. Cut off and discard the stacking gel from the gel electrophoresis using ready-made SDS-gels.
4. Immerse the remaining gel in cathode-buffer (25 mM tris, 40 mM glycine, 10% methanol) for 15 minutes.
5. Incubate transfer membranes (Immobilon-P, Millipore) in 100% methanol for 15 seconds, wash in water for 2 minutes, and equilibrate in anode-buffer II (25 mM Tris, 10% methanol) for at least 5 minutes.
6. Incubate three pieces of filter paper in cathode-buffer, two pieces in anode-buffer I (0.3 M Tris, 10% methanol), and one piece with the membrane in anode-buffer II.
7. Assemble the transfer sandwich directly on the anode-plate of the semi-dry blotting device, starting with two filter papers with anode-buffer I, one filter paper with anode-buffer II, membrane, separating gel, and three filter papers with cathode-buffer.
8. Perform transfer at 1.2 mA/cm^2 for 1 hour.
9. Block the membranes with 4% blocking reagent in TBST (20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.6, 137 mM NaCl, 0.1% Tween 20) for 1 hour.
10. Wash the membranes five times: two short immersions, a 15-minute wash, and two 5-minute washing steps.
11. Incubate with primary antibodies at room temperature for 1 hour, followed by the previously described washing procedure.
12. Determine the final concentration for each primary antibody empirically.
13. Incubate the membranes with the secondary antibody (anti-rabbit HRP-conjugate) at a final concentration of 1:500,000, followed by washing steps.
14. Detect reactive bands using ECL detection.
15. For antibody production, raise polyclonal antibodies in rabbits against KLH-conjugated peptides derived from conserved regions of the target protein.
16. Validate the antibodies using positive and negative controls.

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation, including protein concentration determination, gel electrophoresis, membrane transfer, antibody incubation, and detection. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the specific details of the mitochondrial isolation process and the specific concentrations and incubation times for the primary antibodies.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "while the mitochondrial fraction from the 33–80% interface (M2) showed only a weak positive signal for contamination with Golgi vesicles"
2. "The signal strength for the three mitochondrial marker antibodies used, the plant alternative oxidase 1 and 2 (AOX1/2) and the cytochrome coxidase subunit 2 (COX II) which are both localised in the inner mitochondrial membrane, and the antibody against the voltage-dependent anion-selective channel protein 1 (VDAC1), a protein of the outer mitochondrial membrane, was higher for the mitochondrial protein extracts from the M1 fraction."
3. "Integrity of the mitochondria of both fractions was confirmed by the positive immunoblot signal for VDAC1."

**Chloroplast Isolation Steps:**

1. "During the purification in the Percoll density gradient, the intact chloroplasts accumulated at the 40–80% interface, while the broken chloroplasts were found at the 10–40% interface."
2. "To detect impurities in the chloroplast fraction (C), the same set of antibodies as for the mitochondrial fraction was used."
3. "Immunoblots showed that the chloroplasts were free of contamination by Golgi, but were contaminated to some extent with tonoplast membranes."
4. "The signal strength of the mitochondrial marker antibodies against chloroplast proteins varied strongly."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some steps for mitochondrial and chloroplast isolation. The mitochondrial isolation steps include identifying the mitochondrial fraction, using specific antibodies, and confirming the integrity of the mitochondria. However, the protocol is not fully described, and some details are missing. The chloroplast isolation steps involve identifying intact and broken chloroplasts, using specific antibodies, and detecting impurities. Again, the protocol is not complete, and some steps are unclear.

Previous publications using mitochondria for proteomic

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The isolation protocol for mitochondria in moss protonema revealed two bands of different densities, indicating two populations of mitochondria.
2. The homogenization step using a potter before density gradient purification may cause disruption of some mitochondria.
3. However, the clear signal in the immunoblot obtained for the mitochondrial outer membrane protein VDAC1, as well as the staining with Mitotracker RedCMXRos, indicate that both mitochondrial fractions contain intact mitochondria.
4. Very few reports of a second mitochondrial band in a density gradient exist, suggesting that this is a unique finding in moss.
5. A comparison of these results with other organisms would be far-fetched as there are no such developmental stages in moss.
6. The observations by Segui-Simarro et al (2008) in Arabidopsis were based on reconstructed thin-sections of electron micrographs and not on isolated mitochondria, so they should be considered as an in planta finding.

The two different mitochondrial

nema tissue) or from two subpopulations of mitochondriafrom the same set of cells The results from the immunoblot analysis demonstrate
that the plastidic contamination of both mitochondrialfractions is minimal or not existent, since the signal from
the very abundant light-harvesting complex protein

addition, this difference might be caused by residual con-

mitochondrial markers in both mitochondrial protein frac-tions as compared to the total protein and chloroplast
protein extracts, which is best observed for the AOX1/2

enriched fraction of organelles Interestingly, the signal for212 Plant Cell Rep (2011) 30:205–215

content between the mitochondrial fractions Potential contaminations of the M2 fraction by Golgi

ruled out as the removal of the intact chloroplasts from the

lead to a residual contamination of the glass pipette
surface Immunoblotting results with the urea-extracted proteins

summary: Two mitochondrial fractions in moss were isolated from pro-tonema without protoplastation. It is unclear if these fractions originate from different cell types or subpopulations within the same cells. Immunoblot analysis shows minimal plastidic contamination in both fractions. The signal intensity for mitochondrial markers is stronger in the M1 fraction, suggesting differences in protein content. The chloroplast fraction shows higher contamination with outer mitochondrial membranes than inner mitochondrial membrane proteins. Contamination by Golgi apparatus is absent, but there is a signal from tonoplast contaminants. The extraction method for chloroplast proteins worked well, with clear signals for soluble proteins in the stroma and membrane proteins in the thylakoid. The PsbP marker gave two signals in the immunoblot, one matching the expected size and another unexpected minor band.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article Section: Methods**

**Step 1:** "Our protocol for a simultaneous isolation of chloroplasts and mitochondria from the same biological sample offers several advantages as it will facilitate a direct comparison of organelles under identical physiological conditions."
**Step 2:** "Protonema tissue has only few different cell types and thus is a rather homogeneous material, well suited for systems biology approaches."
**Step 3:** "In addition, the rapid protocol using intact tissue rather than protoplasts described here, will promote such studies in P. patens."
**Step 4:** "Furthermore, employing this protocol we found two distinct mitochondrial bands, a finding that has not been reported before for moss and will be investigated further."
**Step 5:** "The use of plant compartment marker antibodies designed for algae or seed-plants in this study supports two main points. On the one hand, it demonstrates that plant antibodies can be used across species if their target sequences are conserved and, on the other hand, it has shown for this study that both organellar fractions are highly enriched with only minor contaminations by other organelles."
**Step 6:** "Both aspects paired with the good protein yields obtained from the extractions will allow the analysis of sub-cellular proteomes of this non-flowering plant, thus helping to increase our understanding of the evolution of metabolic compartmentalization, of biosynthetic pathways and of protein sorting mechanisms in land plants."

**Conclusion:**
The article describes a protocol for the simultaneous isolation of chloroplasts and mitochondria from protonema tissue of P. patens. The protocol is advantageous because it allows for a direct comparison of organelles under identical physiological conditions. The use of intact tissue instead of protoplasts is a rapid approach that promotes studies in P. patens. The protocol resulted in the identification of two distinct mitochondrial bands, which is a novel finding in moss. The use of plant compartment marker antibodies confirmed the enrichment of organellar fractions with minimal contamination. The protocol also yielded good protein yields, enabling the analysis of sub-cellular proteomes. However, the specific steps for the mitochondrial isolation are not provided in the article, and further investigation is needed to understand the processing or modification of the mitochondrial proteins.

summary: Summary 4: Goulas et al. (2006) identified two putative genes, PsbP-1 and PsbP-2, in A. thaliana. The minor band observed did not correspond to the PsbP-2 protein. They also found a second immunoreactive band at 20 kDa, higher than the expected 18 kDa band using the PsaD antibody. The origin of this second signal remains unclear. The researchers used a protocol for simultaneous isolation of chloroplasts and mitochondria from the same biological sample, allowing for a direct comparison under identical physiological conditions. They utilized intact protonema tissue, which is well-suited for systems biology approaches and promotes studies in P. patens. The study revealed two distinct mitochondrial bands in moss, a novel finding. Plant compartment marker antibodies designed for algae or seed-plants can be used across species if target sequences are conserved. The organellar fractions obtained from the extractions were highly enriched with minimal contaminations, enabling the analysis of sub-cellular proteomes in non-flowering plants. The research was supported by various institutions and initiatives, including the German Federal and State Governments, Spemann Graduate School of Biology and Medicine, German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF), and MINT-Initiative of the State of Baden-Württemberg. The researchers acknowledged the technical assistance of Christine Glockner and the use of Open Access and Creative Commons Attribution Noncommercial License.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "Isolation and subfractionation of mitochondria from plants" by AH Millar, LJ Sweetlove, P Giege, CJ Leaver (2001b): This article provides a protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from plants, including Arabidopsis.

2. "Rapid alteration of the phosphoproteome in the moss Physcomitrella patens after cytokinin treatment" by D Heintz, A Erxleben, AA High, V Wurtz, R Reski, A Van Dorsselaer, E Sarnighausen (2006): This article mentions the isolation of mitochondria from the moss Physcomitrella patens, but does not provide a detailed protocol.

3. "Experimental analysis of the rice mitochondrial proteome, its biogenesis, and heterogeneity" by S Huang, NL Taylor, R Narsai, H Eubel, J Whelan, AH Millar (2009): This article discusses the experimental analysis of the rice mitochondrial proteome, but does not provide a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation.

4. mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** Sweetlove LJ, Taylor NL, Leaver CJ (2007) Isolation of intact, functional mitochondria from the model plant Arabidopsis thaliana Methods Mol Biol 372:125–136

**1. Read Article:**
I have read the article and identified the relevant sections for mitochondrial isolation.

**2. Extract Steps:**
- "Mitochondria were isolated from Arabidopsis thaliana using a differential centrifugation method (Taylor et al. 2002)."
- "Leaves were homogenized in a cold grinding medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM Tricine-KOH (pH 7.5), 5 mM EGTA, 1 mM EDTA, 0.1% (w/v) BSA, 0.1% (w/v) PVP, 2 mM DTT, and 1% (w/v) PEG 3350."
- "The homogenate was filtered through Miracloth and centrifuged at 4°C for 10 min at 1,000 × g."
- "The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 4°C for 10 min at 10,000 × g."
- "The resulting pellet was resuspended in a resuspension medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM Tricine-KOH (pH 7.5), 5 mM EGTA, 1 mM EDTA, 2 mM DTT, and 1% (w/v) PEG 3350."
- "The resuspended pellet was layered onto a discontinuous Percoll gradient consisting of 40% (v/v) and 80% (v/v) Percoll in resuspension medium."
- "The gradient was centrifuged at 4°C for 30 min at 25,000 × g."
- "The mitochondrial fraction was collected from the interface between the 40% and 80% Percoll layers."
- "The collected fraction was diluted with resuspension medium and centrifuged at 4°C for 10 min at 10,000 × g."
- "The resulting pellet was resuspended in resuspension medium and centrifuged at 4°C for 10 min at 10,000 × g."
- "The final pellet containing the isolated mitochondria was resuspended in a storage buffer containing 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM Tricine-KOH (pH 7.5), 5 mM EGTA, 1 mM EDTA, 2 mM DTT, and 1% (w/v) PEG 3350."

**3. Note Incompleteness:**
No missing protocol parts or unclear steps were identified.

**4. Format Steps:**
1. Homogenize leaves in a cold grinding medium.
2. Filter the homogenate and centrifuge at 1,000 × g for 10 min.
3. Collect the supernatant and centrifuge at 10,000 × g for 10 min.
4. Resuspend the resulting pellet in a resuspension medium.
5. Layer the resuspended pellet onto a discontinuous Percoll gradient.
6. Centrifuge the gradient at 25,000 × g for 30 min.
7. Collect the mitochondrial fraction from the interface between the Percoll layers.
8. Dilute the collected fraction and centrifuge at 10,000 × g for 10 min.
9. Resuspend the resulting pellet in resuspension medium.
10. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 10,000 × g for 10 min.
11. Resuspend the final pellet containing the isolated mitochondria in a storage buffer.

**5. Indicate Uncertainties:**
No ambiguous or partially described steps were identified.

**Conclusion:**
The protocol for isolating intact

summary:
1. Microarray analysis reveals conserved transcriptional regulation of salt stress and abscisic acid signaling in the moss Physcomitrella patens.
2. Two genes in Physcomitrella patens encode phage-type RNA polymerases targeted to mitochondria and plastids.
3. The extrinsic proteins of photosystem II play a crucial role in photosynthesis.
4. Chloroplasts and their sub-organellar compartments can be purified and analyzed using specific assays.
5. The mitochondrial cycle in Arabidopsis shoot apical meristem and leaf primordium cells is defined by a perinuclear tentaculate/cage-like mitochondrion.
6. The chloroplast protein import machinery is a complex process.
7. C9-aldehydes are biosynthesized in the moss Physcomitrella patens.
8. Intact and functional mitochondria can be isolated from Arabidopsis thaliana.
9. A method for quantitative protein recovery in the presence of detergents and lipids has been developed.
10. Knocking out a gene encoding sulfite reductase in Physcomitrella patens affects both gametophytic and sporophytic development.
11. The PsbP protein is essential for photosystem II complex assembly and stability in Arabidopsis thaliana.
